channel.title: Terms on emsenn.net
channel.description: Recent content in Terms on emsenn.net

item.title: Acedia
item.description: Acedia is the Thomist name for the vice — one of the seven capital sins in the Western tradition — of spiritual torpor: the inability to rouse oneself to the good one knows one should pursue. Acedia is not laziness in the ordinary sense (the disinclination to work) but a specifically spiritual condition. The acedic soul is restless and distracted, often busy, but withdrawn from its own ends. What it knows would fulfill it has become aversive; what does not fulfill it has become compelling.

item.title: Curiositas
item.description: Curiositas is the Thomist vice of disordered cognitive appetite: the desire to know that has been severed from its proper ends and that consumes its subject without orienting them to truth. Aquinas treats it (II-II, q. 167) as the deformation of an appetite that is good in itself: humans naturally desire to know, the desire is part of their constitution, and the desire becomes vicious when it loses its orientation. The curious subject knows in order to know more; the knowing has become the end. The result is restlessness, scattering, an inability to be present to any one thing because the next thing is always pulling.

item.title: Spes
item.description: Spes is the Thomist theological virtue of hope: the disposition by which the soul tends toward a future good that is difficult but possible to attain, structured as expectation rather than certainty. Where the natural virtues (prudence, justice, fortitude, temperance) order the soul to natural ends within reach of natural reason, the theological virtues (faith, hope, charity) order the soul to a supernatural end beyond natural reach. Spes is the virtue specifically concerned with the temporal structure of the soul’s relation to that end — what it means to expect, to wait, to trust the good is coming without claiming to have it already.

item.title: Studiositas
item.description: Studiositas is the Thomist virtue of studiousness: the disciplined and proportioned love of knowledge that orders the desire to know to the goods that knowledge serves. The cognitive appetite is real — humans desire to know, and the desire is good — but the appetite, like all the appetites, requires moderation. Studiositas is the moderating virtue. It is what makes a knower wise rather than merely informed, oriented rather than scattered, deepened by what they learn rather than diluted by it.
